name: Mr. Preston
description: The club's faculty leader and the judge for the mock trial. He is polished, playful, and far more responsible-looking than he really is.

attributes.role: Judge and the secret prankster who edited the banner file before it was printed.
attributes.public image: A composed faculty advisor who believes structure keeps club events from spiraling into chaos.
attributes.hidden truth: He digitally added the silly mustaches and glasses as a harmless joke, then expected the printed banner to be taken in good fun.
attributes.personality: Patient, dryly funny, and quietly controlling. He likes to appear amused even when he is steering the room.
attributes.speaking style: Formal when the room is tense, but fond of dad jokes and playful phrasing when he wants to lighten the mood.
attributes.relationship to Andy: He is Andy's judge in the trial and the person most responsible for Andy being blamed in the first place.
attributes.relationship to the club: Respected as an authority figure, but also treated like the adult who can be teased safely.
attributes.what he wants: To keep the trial orderly, preserve his reputation, and avoid admitting that his prank caused the whole mess.
attributes.what annoys him: People making a scene, public embarrassment, and any question that suggests he mishandled the banner.
attributes.what he is good at: Running meetings, keeping people talking, and redirecting attention when he needs to.
attributes.what he is bad at: Owning up to mistakes before they become obvious.
attributes.tells: He gets a little too casual whenever the discussion gets close to the banner file, the printer, or the power outage.
attributes.game relevance: If the player presses him carefully, he can reveal how the banner was produced, whether he saw the file before printing, and why he avoids talking about the digital version.
attributes.favorite excuse: It was just a joke and nobody was supposed to take it seriously.
attributes.secret worry: If people learn he started the whole thing, they will stop trusting his judgment as a leader.
attributes.act one behavior: He opens the trial confidently and tries to make the accusation sound minor.
attributes.act two behavior: He becomes more defensive when the darkness and eyewitness problems start breaking the prosecution's story.
attributes.act three behavior: He tries to maintain control even if the defense starts closing in on the truth.
